metadata.title: Economics and Computation - Multiple-Choice Pass-Focused Question Bank
metadata.created_for: Last-minute exam review and passing the course
metadata.study_method: Answer every question without looking at the solution, read every explanation, and repeat each mistake until correct twice.
metadata.important_note: This bank contains only multiple-choice questions and targets the central definitions, formulas, theorem statements, standard examples, and common exam distinctions. It is designed as a pass-focused foundation, not a guarantee of any particular exam result.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes a direct-revelation mechanism?
choices: Agents report private types and the mechanism selects an outcome and payments
Agents choose outcomes without reporting information
The mechanism observes all true values automatically
Only the seller submits a report
questions.answer: Agents report private types and the mechanism selects an outcome and payments
questions.explanation: A direct mechanism asks agents to report their private information and then applies allocation and payment rules.

questions.prompt: For quasilinear preferences, an agent's utility is best written as:
choices: Value for the outcome minus payment
questions.answer: Value for the outcome minus payment
questions.explanation: Quasilinear utility separates value from money: u_i = v_i(x)-p_i.

questions.prompt: Which condition says truthful reporting is optimal regardless of the other agents' reports?
questions.answer: Dominant-strategy incentive compatibility
questions.explanation: DSIC requires truth-telling to be a dominant strategy.

questions.prompt: Ex-post individual rationality means that:
choices: A truthful agent gets nonnegative utility for every realized profile
The mechanism always earns nonnegative revenue
Truthful reporting is a Nash equilibrium only
questions.answer: A truthful agent gets nonnegative utility for every realized profile
questions.explanation: Ex-post IR guarantees nonnegative truthful utility after values are realized.

questions.prompt: Which quantity is normally excluded from social welfare when payments are pure transfers?
choices: Payments between agents and the mechanism
questions.answer: Payments between agents and the mechanism
questions.explanation: Payments redistribute utility and usually cancel from total welfare.

questions.prompt: The revelation principle is useful because it allows the designer to study:
choices: Truthful direct mechanisms instead of arbitrary indirect implementations
questions.answer: Truthful direct mechanisms instead of arbitrary indirect implementations
questions.explanation: It reduces many implementation questions to truthful direct mechanisms.

questions.prompt: A mechanism is composed primarily of:
choices: An allocation rule and a payment rule
A strategy set and a probability density only
A reserve price and a graph
A utility function chosen by the seller
questions.answer: An allocation rule and a payment rule
questions.explanation: The allocation chooses the outcome and the payment rule determines transfers.

questions.prompt: Which statement correctly distinguishes value and utility?
choices: Value measures benefit from the outcome; utility subtracts payment
Utility is always larger than value
questions.answer: Value measures benefit from the outcome; utility subtracts payment
questions.explanation: Under quasilinear preferences, utility equals value minus payment.

questions.prompt: If a bidder values an item at 12, wins, and pays 7, her utility is:
questions.explanation: The utility is 12-7=5.

questions.prompt: A mechanism is welfare maximizing when it selects:
choices: A feasible outcome with maximum total reported value
The outcome with maximum total payment
The outcome preferred by the seller only
An arbitrary outcome with nonnegative revenue
questions.answer: A feasible outcome with maximum total reported value
questions.explanation: Welfare maximization chooses the feasible allocation with largest total value.

questions.prompt: In a single-parameter environment, each agent's private type is:
choices: A complete ranking over all outcomes
questions.answer: One scalar value or cost
questions.explanation: Single-parameter types are summarized by one number.

questions.prompt: For a value-maximization bidder, monotonicity requires that increasing her bid cannot:
choices: Turn a winning outcome into a losing one, holding others fixed
questions.answer: Turn a winning outcome into a losing one, holding others fixed
questions.explanation: A monotone allocation preserves winning when a bidder raises her bid.

questions.prompt: The critical value of a winning bidder is:
choices: The smallest report at which she would still win
questions.answer: The smallest report at which she would still win
questions.explanation: It is the threshold separating winning from losing.

questions.prompt: A normalized truthful single-parameter mechanism charges a winner:
choices: Her own bid in every case
questions.answer: Her critical value
questions.explanation: Threshold payments support DSIC when the allocation rule is monotone.

questions.prompt: In a Vickrey single-item auction with bids 15, 11, and 4, the winner pays:
questions.explanation: The highest bidder wins and pays the second-highest bid.

questions.prompt: Why is bidding above one's value risky in a second-price auction?
choices: One may win and pay more than the item is worth
It changes the second-highest bid to zero
questions.answer: One may win and pay more than the item is worth
questions.explanation: Overbidding can create negative utility when the price exceeds true value.

questions.prompt: Why can bidding below one's value be harmful in a second-price auction?
choices: One may lose an item that would have yielded positive utility
It makes every other bidder truthful
questions.answer: One may lose an item that would have yielded positive utility
questions.explanation: Underbidding can cause a profitable winning opportunity to be missed.

questions.prompt: A first-price auction encourages bid shading because:
choices: The winner pays her own bid
The winner pays the second-highest bid
The allocation is independent of bids
questions.answer: The winner pays her own bid
questions.explanation: Bidders trade off winning probability against a lower payment.

questions.prompt: Which pair characterizes truthful normalized single-parameter mechanisms?
questions.answer: Monotone allocation and critical-value payments
questions.explanation: This is the central characterization for deterministic single-parameter mechanisms.

questions.prompt: Algorithmic mechanism design becomes difficult when:
choices: The desired allocation problem is computationally hard
questions.answer: The desired allocation problem is computationally hard
questions.explanation: Truthfulness must be combined with computationally feasible allocation.

questions.prompt: A multi-parameter type differs from a single-parameter type because it:
choices: May assign different values to many different outcomes
questions.answer: May assign different values to many different outcomes
questions.explanation: Multi-parameter agents have a valuation vector or function.

questions.prompt: VCG selects the feasible outcome maximizing:
questions.answer: The sum of reported valuations
questions.explanation: VCG uses welfare maximization as its allocation rule.

questions.prompt: Under Clarke pivot payments, agent i pays:
choices: Best welfare achievable by others without i minus others' welfare in the chosen outcome
The total welfare of all agents
questions.answer: Best welfare achievable by others without i minus others' welfare in the chosen outcome
questions.explanation: The payment equals the externality imposed on the others.

questions.prompt: If an agent's presence does not change the welfare attainable by others, her VCG payment is:
choices: The second-highest value in every setting
questions.answer: Zero
questions.explanation: No externality implies no pivot payment.

questions.prompt: In a single-item setting, the VCG winner's externality equals:
choices: The highest value among the losing bidders
questions.answer: The highest value among the losing bidders
questions.explanation: Without the winner, the best loser would receive the item.

questions.prompt: Which property is guaranteed by standard VCG under quasilinear preferences?
choices: Strong budget balance in every problem
questions.answer: Dominant-strategy truthfulness
questions.explanation: VCG is DSIC but need not maximize revenue or be computationally easy.

questions.prompt: Why can VCG be computationally impractical?
choices: It may require solving a hard welfare-optimization problem several times
Its payment formula never uses optimization
It cannot handle more than two agents
It requires agents to reveal no information
questions.answer: It may require solving a hard welfare-optimization problem several times
questions.explanation: Computing the allocation and counterfactual welfare can be hard.

questions.prompt: Which statement about VCG and revenue is correct?
choices: VCG maximizes welfare, not generally revenue
VCG always produces the largest possible revenue
VCG is identical to first-price bidding
questions.answer: VCG maximizes welfare, not generally revenue
questions.explanation: Revenue optimality is a different design objective.

questions.prompt: In a combinatorial auction, a bidder's value for {A,B} can differ from the sum of values for A and B because of:
questions.answer: Complements or substitutes
questions.explanation: Bundle valuations can capture complementarities and substitution.

questions.prompt: The key intuition behind VCG truthfulness is that each agent's utility is aligned with:
choices: Total welfare up to a term independent of her report
questions.answer: Total welfare up to a term independent of her report
questions.explanation: The pivot payment removes the agent's effect on others into an externality term.

questions.prompt: Revenue-maximizing auction design usually requires:
choices: A probabilistic model of bidders' values
Knowledge of every realized value in advance
questions.answer: A probabilistic model of bidders' values
questions.explanation: Bayesian auction design uses known value distributions.

questions.prompt: For one bidder offered price r, expected revenue is:
choices: r times the probability that value is at least r
r plus the bidder's expected value
questions.answer: r times the probability that value is at least r
questions.explanation: Revenue is r upon sale and zero otherwise.

questions.prompt: A reserve price primarily serves to:
choices: Prevent sale when the highest acceptable bid is too low
questions.answer: Prevent sale when the highest acceptable bid is too low
questions.explanation: Withholding the item can improve expected revenue.

questions.prompt: Myerson's virtual value for distribution F with density f is:
questions.explanation: This is the standard virtual valuation formula.

questions.prompt: A distribution is regular when:
questions.answer: Its virtual value is nondecreasing
questions.explanation: Regularity makes virtual-surplus maximization monotone.

questions.prompt: Myerson's lemma connects expected revenue with expected:
questions.answer: Virtual welfare
questions.explanation: Expected payments equal expected virtual surplus under the standard conditions.

questions.prompt: In the regular single-item optimal auction, the item is allocated to:
choices: The bidder with the highest nonnegative virtual value
The bidder with the lowest value
The bidder with the highest payment after allocation
questions.answer: The bidder with the highest nonnegative virtual value
questions.explanation: Negative virtual values lead the seller to withhold the item.

questions.prompt: For i.i.d. regular bidders, the optimal single-item auction is often described as:
choices: A Vickrey auction with an appropriate reserve price
A first-price auction with no reserve
questions.answer: A Vickrey auction with an appropriate reserve price
questions.explanation: The monopoly reserve augments the second-price auction.

questions.prompt: Which statement best compares welfare and revenue?
choices: The welfare-maximizing allocation can differ from the revenue-maximizing allocation
They always select the same outcome
Welfare uses virtual values by definition
questions.answer: The welfare-maximizing allocation can differ from the revenue-maximizing allocation
questions.explanation: Revenue optimization can reject low positive values because virtual values may be negative.

questions.prompt: Simple near-optimal auctions are studied because:
choices: Exactly optimal auctions can be complex or fragile
Simple auctions require no incentive analysis
questions.answer: Exactly optimal auctions can be complex or fragile
questions.explanation: Approximation can trade a little revenue for simplicity and robustness.

questions.prompt: A normal-form game consists of:
choices: Players, strategy sets, and payoff functions
A graph and a reserve price
questions.answer: Players, strategy sets, and payoff functions
questions.explanation: These ingredients specify strategic interaction.

questions.prompt: A pure strategy chooses:
questions.answer: One action with probability one
questions.explanation: Pure strategies are deterministic actions.

questions.prompt: A mixed strategy is:
choices: A probability distribution over pure strategies
questions.answer: A probability distribution over pure strategies
questions.explanation: Mixed strategies randomize among pure actions.

questions.prompt: A strategy is a best response when it:
choices: Maximizes the player's payoff given the others' strategies
questions.answer: Maximizes the player's payoff given the others' strategies
questions.explanation: Best responses are conditional on opponents' behavior.

questions.prompt: At a Nash equilibrium:
choices: Every player's strategy is a best response to the others
questions.answer: Every player's strategy is a best response to the others
questions.explanation: No player has a profitable unilateral deviation.

questions.prompt: Which statement follows from Nash's theorem?
choices: Every finite game has at least one mixed Nash equilibrium
Every finite game has a pure Nash equilibrium
questions.answer: Every finite game has at least one mixed Nash equilibrium
questions.explanation: Existence is guaranteed when mixed strategies are allowed.

questions.prompt: Matching pennies has no pure Nash equilibrium because:
choices: At every pure profile, one player wants to deviate
The game has only one strategy per player
questions.answer: At every pure profile, one player wants to deviate
questions.explanation: No pure profile is stable under unilateral deviation.

questions.prompt: A strictly dominant strategy is one that:
choices: Gives a higher payoff than every alternative against every opponents' profile
Is optimal only at one equilibrium
questions.answer: Gives a higher payoff than every alternative against every opponents' profile
questions.explanation: Dominance is stronger than being a best response at one profile.

questions.prompt: In a zero-sum two-player game:
choices: One player's gain equals the other's loss
questions.answer: One player's gain equals the other's loss
questions.explanation: The two payoffs sum to a constant, usually normalized to zero.

questions.prompt: A correlated equilibrium differs from a Nash equilibrium because:
choices: Players may condition actions on recommendations from a correlation device
questions.answer: Players may condition actions on recommendations from a correlation device
questions.explanation: Correlation permits coordinated recommendations while preserving obedience incentives.

questions.prompt: In nonatomic selfish routing, each traveler is modeled as:
choices: Infinitesimally small relative to total flow
A bidder with a reserve price
questions.answer: Infinitesimally small relative to total flow
questions.explanation: An individual traveler does not noticeably change edge latency alone.

questions.prompt: A Wardrop equilibrium requires that:
choices: Every used path has minimum latency among available paths
The total latency is globally minimal
Unused paths have lower latency than used paths
questions.answer: Every used path has minimum latency among available paths
questions.explanation: No infinitesimal user can improve by switching routes.

questions.prompt: The social cost of an edge with flow x_e and latency l_e(x_e) is:
questions.answer: x_e l_e(x_e)
questions.explanation: Total delay on an edge equals number of users times delay per user.

questions.prompt: Price of anarchy for a cost-minimization game is:
choices: Worst equilibrium cost divided by optimal cost
Optimal cost divided by worst equilibrium cost
Total flow divided by path count
questions.answer: Worst equilibrium cost divided by optimal cost
questions.explanation: PoA quantifies the inefficiency of selfish behavior.

questions.prompt: A price of anarchy of 4/3 means:
choices: The worst equilibrium cost can be 33.3% above optimum
The optimum is four times the equilibrium
Only three of four agents travel
questions.answer: The worst equilibrium cost can be 33.3% above optimum
questions.explanation: The ratio compares equilibrium cost with optimal cost.

questions.prompt: In the Pigou network with one unit of demand, latencies x and 1, the Wardrop equilibrium sends:
choices: All flow on the edge with latency x
Half the flow on each edge
All flow on the constant edge
questions.answer: All flow on the edge with latency x
questions.explanation: At total flow one, both used-path latency conditions support all flow on x, yielding cost one.

questions.prompt: In the same Pigou example, the socially optimal split places:
choices: Half the flow on each edge
questions.answer: Half the flow on each edge
questions.explanation: Minimizing x^2+(1-x) yields x=1/2.

questions.prompt: Why does selfish routing become inefficient?
choices: Users ignore the delay they impose on others
Every user pays a VCG price
questions.answer: Users ignore the delay they impose on others
questions.explanation: The unpriced congestion externality separates private and social costs.

questions.prompt: A marginal-cost toll on edge e is based on:
choices: x_e times the derivative of latency
questions.answer: x_e times the derivative of latency
questions.explanation: The toll charges the external congestion cost x_e l'_e(x_e).

questions.prompt: Braess's paradox shows that:
choices: Adding a road can worsen equilibrium travel time
questions.answer: Adding a road can worsen equilibrium travel time
questions.explanation: More capacity can alter incentives and create a worse equilibrium.

questions.prompt: An exact potential function has the property that:
choices: A unilateral payoff change equals the corresponding potential change
It is constant across all profiles
It exists in every finite game
questions.answer: A unilateral payoff change equals the corresponding potential change
questions.explanation: Exact potentials track unilateral incentives exactly.

questions.prompt: Why must strict better-response dynamics terminate in a finite potential game?
choices: The potential strictly improves and only finitely many profiles exist
Every player has a dominant strategy
questions.answer: The potential strictly improves and only finitely many profiles exist
questions.explanation: Strict improvement cannot cycle through finitely many potential values.

questions.prompt: A local maximum of an exact potential is:
questions.answer: A pure Nash equilibrium
questions.explanation: No unilateral deviation can improve payoff at a potential local maximum.

questions.prompt: Rosenthal's potential is associated with:
questions.answer: Congestion games
questions.explanation: Rosenthal proved that congestion games are potential games.

questions.prompt: In an atomic congestion game, a player's cost depends on:
choices: The resources she uses and how many players use them
questions.answer: The resources she uses and how many players use them
questions.explanation: Resource congestion determines individual costs.

questions.prompt: Which statement is true of every finite exact potential game?
choices: It has at least one pure Nash equilibrium
questions.answer: It has at least one pure Nash equilibrium
questions.explanation: A global potential maximum is a pure Nash equilibrium.

questions.prompt: Best-response dynamics can cycle in a general finite game because:
choices: There may be no global potential that improves with every move
questions.answer: There may be no global potential that improves with every move
questions.explanation: Potential structure is what rules out improvement cycles.

questions.prompt: A congestion game's potential is generally:
choices: Not identical to total social cost
questions.answer: Not identical to total social cost
questions.explanation: Rosenthal potential is a separate function used to track incentives.

questions.prompt: Finite improvement property means:
choices: Every sequence of strict unilateral improvements is finite
questions.answer: Every sequence of strict unilateral improvements is finite
questions.explanation: FIP is the convergence property induced by ordinal potentials.

questions.prompt: An ordinal potential needs to preserve:
choices: The sign of unilateral payoff improvements
questions.answer: The sign of unilateral payoff improvements
questions.explanation: Ordinal potentials rise whenever a player makes a strict improvement.

questions.prompt: External regret compares actual performance with:
choices: The best single fixed action in hindsight
The best changing action each round
The social optimum of the game
questions.answer: The best single fixed action in hindsight
questions.explanation: External regret uses one fixed benchmark action.

questions.prompt: A learning algorithm is no-external-regret when:
choices: Average external regret converges to zero
Total regret is always exactly zero
It converges to a pure Nash equilibrium every time
questions.answer: Average external regret converges to zero
questions.explanation: Sublinear total regret implies vanishing regret per round.

questions.prompt: If total regret is O(sqrt(T)), average regret is:
questions.answer: O(1/sqrt(T))
questions.explanation: Divide total regret by T.

questions.prompt: Multiplicative weights assigns more future probability to:
choices: Actions that have performed relatively well
Only the action played in round one
questions.answer: Actions that have performed relatively well
questions.explanation: Weights are updated using observed rewards or losses.

questions.prompt: The learning-rate parameter controls:
choices: How aggressively weights react to feedback
The number of players in the game
Whether the game has a Nash equilibrium
questions.answer: How aggressively weights react to feedback
questions.explanation: Large learning rates react quickly; small ones update gradually.

questions.prompt: When every player has vanishing external regret, empirical play approaches:
questions.answer: A coarse correlated equilibrium
questions.explanation: External no-regret corresponds to CCE.

questions.prompt: Coarse correlated equilibrium prevents profitable deviations that are:
choices: Chosen in advance as fixed alternative actions
questions.answer: Chosen in advance as fixed alternative actions
questions.explanation: CCE uses unconditional fixed deviations.

questions.prompt: No-swap-regret learning is connected to:
questions.answer: Correlated equilibrium
questions.explanation: Swap regret controls recommendation-dependent deviations and yields CE.

questions.prompt: Which statement about no-regret play is correct?
choices: The time-average can converge even if period-by-period actions keep changing
Every round must be socially optimal
Players need full knowledge of future losses
questions.answer: The time-average can converge even if period-by-period actions keep changing
questions.explanation: Convergence applies to empirical distributions, not necessarily realized actions.

questions.prompt: The experts setting is useful because it models:
choices: Repeated choice among actions under uncertain or adversarial feedback
questions.answer: Repeated choice among actions under uncertain or adversarial feedback
questions.explanation: Online learning repeatedly selects among experts or actions.

questions.prompt: Mechanism design without money is relevant when:
choices: Transfers are unavailable, illegal, or inappropriate
questions.answer: Transfers are unavailable, illegal, or inappropriate
questions.explanation: Many allocation and social-choice settings cannot use payments.

questions.prompt: In house allocation, the mechanism assigns:
questions.answer: Indivisible houses to agents
questions.explanation: House allocation is a canonical no-money assignment problem.

questions.prompt: A matching is stable when there is no:
choices: Blocking pair that prefer each other to their assignments
questions.answer: Blocking pair that prefer each other to their assignments
questions.explanation: A blocking pair would undermine the proposed matching.

questions.prompt: Student-proposing deferred acceptance is strategy-proof for:
questions.answer: Students
questions.explanation: The proposing side can truthfully report preferences as a dominant strategy.

questions.prompt: Top trading cycles is commonly used for:
questions.answer: House allocation with initial ownership
questions.explanation: TTC reallocates houses through cycles of pointing.

questions.prompt: In one-dimensional facility location with distance costs, choosing the median is:
questions.answer: Strategy-proof
questions.explanation: An agent cannot beneficially move the median by misreporting.

questions.prompt: The Gibbard-Satterthwaite theorem concerns deterministic voting with:
choices: At least three alternatives and unrestricted preferences
questions.answer: At least three alternatives and unrestricted preferences
questions.explanation: Under onto and strategy-proofness, such a rule must be dictatorial.

questions.prompt: A dictatorship in social choice selects:
choices: The top-ranked alternative of one fixed agent
The majority winner in every profile
questions.answer: The top-ranked alternative of one fixed agent
questions.explanation: One agent's ranking determines the outcome.

questions.prompt: Random serial dictatorship is:
choices: A randomized assignment rule based on a random agent order
questions.answer: A randomized assignment rule based on a random agent order
questions.explanation: Agents choose in a random priority order.

questions.prompt: Restricting preferences to a structured domain can help because:
choices: It may permit non-dictatorial strategy-proof rules
It eliminates all strategic behavior automatically
It makes every rule welfare maximizing
questions.answer: It may permit non-dictatorial strategy-proof rules
questions.explanation: Impossibility results can weaken on restricted domains such as single-peaked preferences.
